title: Russia-Ukraine war: Zelensky sacks top army commander after days of protests
description: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has sacked the country's army commander-in-chief, Oleksandr Syrskyi.His dismissal comes just days after the rem
body: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has sacked the country's army commander-in-chief, Oleksandr Syrskyi.His dismissal comes just days after the removal of popular Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov - a move that triggered street protests across the country.Initial rumours that the end of Fedorov's tenure was related to a conflict between him and Syrskyi were soon confirmed.Fedorov, 35, has been widely praised for energising the ministry and transforming the armed forces, as well as leading a drive against corruption. Syrskyi, 60, is seen by many in Ukraine as a Soviet-school commander reluctant to implement radical changes in the army.He was replaced by Mykhailo Drapatyi, who is a popular, battle-hardened general.In a video post on social media late on Tuesday, Zelensky thanked Syrskyi for his military campaigns, including his role in successfully defending the capital Kyiv during the first days of Russia's full-scale invasion in 2022.Zelensky also revealed that earlier in the day, he met Fedorov and offered him a "worthy position in power", but did not say whether the offer had been accepted.Fedorov was last week replaced by Yevhenii Khmara, a former top official at Ukraine's Security Service (SBU). "All decisions will be formalised tomorrow," Zelensky added in his post.Meanwhile, Fedorov congratulated Drapatyi with his new job, describing his appointment as a "new hope in the fight of free people for freedom and justice".He said it was "the voice of change that could not be ignored".Fedorov also revealed that he had called Syrskyi to thank him for the defence of the Kyiv region and other "historic battles"."But we need to move even faster and write new chapters correcting all previous mistakes," he added.Fedorov, 35, had been appointed defence minister only in January - but has been widely praised for using data to analyse and try to improve performance on the front line.A former minister of digital transformation, he was active from the early days of the full-scale invasion by setting up a volunteer "IT Army of Ukraine" to launch cyber-attacks against Russia.Later, he led a successful fundraising campaign called the Army of Drones and brought in elements of "gamification" to the war, designing a system that awarded Ukrainian military units with credits for hitting Russian assets.Syrskyi has so far not publicly commented on the latest developments.On Monday, he wrote an op-ed for the Militarny website, saying of his relationship with Fedorov: "I perceived our relationship as a working one"."If I offended anyone, Mykhailo Albertovych [Fedorov], I'm sorry."On the protests that have been held in several Ukrainian cities, the general wrote that the issues of military contracts, procurement and mobilisation currently being aired in the press and boiled down to pithy slogans by protesters were a great deal more complicated than most people realised."Decisions on which people's lives depend are not made with slogans," he said. "Real work, not the public drama surrounding it, wins the war," Syrskyi wrote.

schema.headline: Russia-Ukraine war: Zelensky sacks top army commander after days of protests

title: Sackings, cabinet surprises and policies: Andy Burnham's first 24 hours as PM
description: Shortly after Andy Burnham telephoned a number of Keir Starmer's allies to sack them from the cabinet on Monday afternoon, John Healey received the si
body: Shortly after Andy Burnham telephoned a number of Keir Starmer's allies to sack them from the cabinet on Monday afternoon, John Healey received the signal to head to Number 10.Wearing a suit and one of his many red ties, the former defence secretary left parliament alone via a back entrance next to a pub."I'm on my way to a meeting," he said with a slight smile as he marched down Whitehall.Healey's imminent appointment as chancellor was a surprise - to him, to his Labour colleagues, and to the British public.It was a consequential moment among a flurry of announcements from the new prime minister, including today's cut in VAT on energy bills, that demonstrate the new government's intention to move at breakneck speed - at least for now.The Conservatives support that tax cut but have questioned its funding.Five weeks earlier Healey had weakened Keir Starmer's grip on Downing Street by quitting as defence secretary in a row over military spending.Soon he would be back around the cabinet table alongside two others whose recent resignations had also damaged Starmer - new defence secretary Wes Streeting and new energy secretary Miatta Fahnbulleh.Were they rewarded for rebelling and hastening Burnham's path to office? Possibly. But all three are also widely seen as skilled operators taking on complex roles.John Healey's elevation to the second most important job in government has delighted senior figures in the Ministry of Defence, who are relieved to have someone in the Treasury who is committed to increasing military spending. "It's brilliant for us," said an insider.Yet the same MOD source is also relieved Healey is not returning. "We were dreading him coming back", they said. "He's a difficult person".Even some senior Conservatives think Healey's appointment as chancellor was clever. "For a number of reasons", explains one former Tory cabinet minister. "He's not Ed Miliband, he's hard to attack, and he's Treasury-literate." Healey served as a minister in the department for five years during Tony Blair's premiership.Burnham ally Anneliese Midgeley has the difficult task of trying to keep Labour MPs - including those sacked as ministers - loyal to the government.Former chief secretary to the prime minister Darren Jones's public swipe at Burnham's energy bills policy is a sign not all will play nice.One Labour MP claims that those sacked by Burnham can be divided into three groups. "Some went because of their politics, others just weren't good, and then there are those disliked in the parliamentary Labour party," said the MP. Behind the scenes, many in Westminster are talking about the possibility of Burnham taking advantage of a possible bounce in the opinion polls and calling a snap general election.Nigel Farage wants one and argues it is necessary since Burnham has no mandate.But with the Reform leader facing both a by-election and questions over his finances, some in his party worry that if Burnham moves too fast, Farage could miss his one great chance to become PM."We are really worried about a snap election," said the Reform source, grinning. "Which is why we're calling for a snap election."Across all the parties at Westminster, plans are being put in place in case a snap contest is called. "We've started preparing," said a senior Liberal Democrat aide.Burnham has appeared to rule out heading to the polls. It would certainly anger some Labour MPs who could lose their seats. Yet it would also offer the new PM an opportunity to get a fresh mandate for more radical policies, as well as secure his position for a full five year term."There is economic pain coming down the horizon", says the former Conservative cabinet minister. "And it's about how far he [Burnham] gets ahead of it. He faces a choice between going for an election, and a slow decline as we saw with Gordon Brown."That former PM was dubbed "bottler Brown" in 2007 after considering going to the polls before deciding against it.Yet Brown got to Number 10 with a mixture of patience, caution, guile and ruthlessness, whereas Burnham's speedy rise was largely due to one major electoral gamble.After his success in Makerfield and then the Labour leadership contest, will Burnham fancy another throw of the dice?

schema.headline: Sackings, cabinet surprises and policies: Andy Burnham's first 24 hours as PM

title: Burnham tells cabinet to tackle living costs at first meeting
description: Image source, Getty ImagesByPaul SeddonPolitical reporter and Chris MasonPolitical editorUpdated 49 minutes agoAndy Burnham has promised to lead a "co
body: Image source, Getty ImagesByPaul SeddonPolitical reporter and Chris MasonPolitical editorUpdated 49 minutes agoAndy Burnham has promised to lead a "cost of living government," as he chaired his first cabinet meeting as prime minister.He told his newly-assembled top team that he wanted to make living expenses a key priority, and give people a "sense that help is coming" on costs.It comes after he announced a VAT cut to household energy bills from October, in his first major announcement since taking office.But his plan to pay for the policy by scrapping Sir Keir Starmer's digital ID scheme led to an early row over whether the plan was properly funded.Downing Street said the estimated £850m cost of the VAT cut over the rest of the financial year could come from £1.8bn earmarked by the previous government to roll out the digital ID scheme in the next three years.But Darren Jones, who was among a clutch of Starmer loyalists sacked from the cabinet on Monday, said the ID programme was "unfunded" and Burnham would need to set out where the money would be found.Opposition parties also seized on the announcement, pointing out the government's budget watchdog has previously said the money to fund digital ID was due to be found from within existing budgets."We are only one day in and already Labour are playing fast and loose with the public finances," said Conservative shadow chancellor Sir Mel Stride.The PM's spokesman insisted the policy would be funded by redirecting "savings" identified by departments to cover the cost of the ID scheme.Sarwar set for minister jobBurnham was given a round of applause by his senior ministers earlier, as he opened the first meeting of his new cabinet after appointing his top team on Monday.Appointments below cabinet rank are expected to follow on Tuesday evening, including Scottish Labour leader Anas Sarwar, who is set to be given a seat in the House of Lords so he can join Burnham's government.It is thought Sarwar, who rocked Starmer's premiership when he called for him to resign in February, will become a business minister.New rules mean he would have to quit his seat in the Scottish Parliament, although a by-election to replace him will not be required as he was elected on the regional list system.Monday's wide-ranging reshuffle saw Starmer loyalists such as Rachel Reeves and David Lammy out of government, with Ed Miliband promoted to foreign secretary and Burnham ally Louise Haigh given a big job running the Cabinet Office.Miatta Fahnbulleh, a Burnham ally promoted into the cabinet as energy secretary, denied he had led a purge of Starmer supporters, telling BBC Newsnight "no one has a right" to be in the cabinet, which contained a "diversity of views".Speaking to staff at the Treasury, new chancellor John Healey said discipline over tax and spending would be his "first duty", as he recommitted to the tax and spending rules set by his predecessor.But he added that government would seek to make the "fullest possible use of public investment" within the framework, alongside making the most of private and international investment.Image source, PA MediaImage caption, Healey gave an address to Treasury staff after his surprise appointment on MondaySpeaking at the start of the cabinet meeting attended by broadcasters, Burnham said the cost of living would be an early priority, with further measures to be announced in the coming days.He said he would be asking ministers to come up with suggestions to lower costs to households, adding: "There are things can be done, big and small."He insisted his government would always show "how we are going to pay for" policies, adding "discipline" on spending would be required.But he conceded that funding new announcements ahead of his first Budget, where big tax decisions are made, would require ministers to be "prepared to reprioritise" how they allocate their budgets.This was also reflected in Burnham's first announcement on Monday, a further £340m to tackle rough sleeping over five years, which is expected to be funded within "uncommitted" spending at the housing department.In a later development on Tuesday, Burnham agreed to allow British military bases to be used for US defensive strikes against Iran - a continuation of the same policy under Sir Keir's government.Sources stressed bases at Diego Garcia and RAF Fairford would only be used for what the UK deems to be defensive operations.The announcement on energy bills, which will see VAT on household electricity reduced from 5% to zero in England, Wales and Scotland, is estimated to save the typical household about £45 a year.It will not apply in Northern Ireland due to post-Brexit rules agreed by the previous Conservative government, with ministers at Stormont instead offered extra money from the Treasury to use on cost of living measures.The Conservatives and Reform UK have both previously called for the measure, with Nigel Farage calling it a benefit of Brexit but insisting it would not "address the real issue of the cost of living" and taking aim at green levies on bills.Meanwhile, further details have been confirmed of Burnham's plan for a new Downing Street unit in Manchester, known as No 10 North.The government says the unit, expected to be initially set up in a building currently housing intelligence service GCHQ, will be given control of aspects of local economic growth and devolution strategy.Alongside this, a new Office for the Prime Minister and the Cabinet will be created to ensure departments remain focused on "top strategic priorities", according to a statement announcing the changes, external.Civil servants working in London have been offered relocation packages to encourage them to move north, it is understood, with Downing Street saying Burnham would try to work from No 10 North "at least one day a week".Additional reporting by Harry Farley.

schema.headline: Burnham tells cabinet to tackle living costs at first meeting

title: Ryanair broken window may have been caused by 'foreign object'
description: An incident on a Ryanair flight which saw a passenger partially sucked out of a plane window may have been caused by "foreign object damage" to an eng
body: An incident on a Ryanair flight which saw a passenger partially sucked out of a plane window may have been caused by "foreign object damage" to an engine, said the airline's boss.Michael O'Leary said an initial investigation into the incident on 10 July between Greece and Germany suggests "it looks like a foreign object damage to the engine on takeoff at Thessaloniki but ... we can't say that definitively".He insisted that the engine on the 18-year-old Boeing 737 plane had been fully serviced and overhauled in the past two years.Serbian national Ljubisa Karović's head and right shoulder were sucked out of the plane's window, leaving him "seriously injured and in shock".His wife Svetlana Grković Maksimović told BBC Serbia that she and two other passengers held on to his legs for several minutes. She said: "We pulled him back together. His entire face was deformed and blood was pouring from his nose and mouth."He was taken to hospital after the plane made an emergency landing.Passengers told local media they heard "some kind of explosion" on board.The aircraft was operated by Ryanair's subsidiary Malta Air.Confirmation that the damage was caused by an external object could affect Ryanair or Boeing's liabilities over the incident.The US National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) is investigating. O'Leary said that a draft report would be issued in about 28 days.

schema.headline: Ryanair broken window may have been caused by 'foreign object'

title: Ann Widdecombe was hit on head 21 times with hammer, prosecutors say
description: Ann Widdecombe was hit on the head with a hammer 21 times at her home as she ate lunch in an attack that was caught on camera, prosecutors have told W
body: Ann Widdecombe was hit on the head with a hammer 21 times at her home as she ate lunch in an attack that was caught on camera, prosecutors have told Westminster Magistrates' Court.Prosecutor Kashif Malik said the man charged with her murder, Joshua Kerry, arrived at her house on 8 July in a red Vauxhall Corsa and entered through the front door.Kerry, who prosecutors say was in the property for two minutes and was wearing black gloves, then allegedly hit Widdecombe repeatedly with a hammer in her kitchen, tipped her from her chair and took her wallet before driving off.The 28-year-old from South Yorkshire later also appeared at the Old Bailey and was remanded in custody.At the Old Bailey hearing, prosecutor Alistair Richardson said the attack on Widdecombe had been carried out with a "ferocity of violence". Malik told the court that Widdecombe did not attend a pre-arranged online interview with Channel 5's Matt Allwright show that had been scheduled for just before 13:00 BST on 8 July. The show's production team tried to contact Widdecombe through her personal assistant, who then asked Widdecombe's gardener to check on her, the court heard.Prosecutors said the gardener entered the house via the open front door on 9 July and found Widdecombe lying face down on her kitchen floor with a serious head injury.Malik told the chief magistrate that security camera footage from her home in Haytor, Devon, showed she had been fatally attacked the day before.He added that police were still investigating whether there was a terrorism connection or political motivation for the attack.Kerry, from Rotherham, was charged with one count of murder on Monday night following an investigation led by counter-terrorism detectives.He appeared emotionless in the dock and spoke only to confirm his name, date of birth and address during the short hearing at Westminster Magistrates' Court. He was not asked to indicate a plea.Later on Tuesday, he also appeared at the Old Bailey. It is expected that he will be remanded at HMP Belmarsh in south-east London.He will next appear at the same court on 9 October for a case management hearing. A provisional trial date has been listed for 8 June 2027.The court heard that a pathologist had given a provisional cause of Widdecombe's death as a blunt force injury to the head.An earlier inquest hearing, that was opened and adjourned on Tuesday, heard that emergency services were called on the day Widdecombe was found, but she was pronounced dead at 12:20.The hearing was also told that while a post-mortem examination had taken place, "at this stage the precise medical cause of death has yet to be established".Widdecombe's career in politics spanned decades.She served as Conservative MP for Maidstone in Kent for 23 years and was appointed to ministerial roles in Sir John Major's government in the 1990s.Following her departure from the Commons, Widdecombe appeared on the BBC's Strictly Come Dancing in 2010 and was a runner-up on Celebrity Big Brother eight years later.She later represented South West England as a Member of the European Parliament (MEP) for the Brexit Party and in 2023 she joined Reform UK as a party spokeswoman.

schema.headline: Ann Widdecombe was hit on head 21 times with hammer, prosecutors say

title: Brazil's Amazon sees sharp decline of area burned in 2025
description: In 2024, the Amazon rainforest experienced its worst forest fires in over two decades, which the Copernicus Climate Change Service put down to "climat
body: In 2024, the Amazon rainforest experienced its worst forest fires in over two decades, which the Copernicus Climate Change Service put down to "climate extremes and human activity". "The 2025 figures are significant and show that it is possible to reduce the burned area in the country," Vera Arruda, a researcher at the Amazon Environmental Research Institute (IPAM) and co-ordinator at MapBiomas, told the BBC."The 31% drop compared to the historical average - driven primarily by the lowest number of fires recorded in the Amazon in 41 years - is positive news, but it does not yet allow us to conclude that this is an established trend or to scale back prevention efforts," Arruda added. The MapBiomas report shows a dramatic drop in area burned not just in the Amazon, but across the whole of Brazil in 2025. Figures show around 12.7 million hectares burned in Brazil in 2025, compared to 30 million hectares in 2024.The highest number of hectares burned in Brazil since records began was in 2007, when 30.7 million were recorded across the country.  The decline in the 2025 figures partly reflects an easing of the unprecedented fires of 2024, helped by cooler La Niña conditions instead of the warmer El Niño.But MapBiomas warned the natural weather pattern of El Niño, which pushes up global temperatures, could extend this year's dry season and increase the risk of forest fires.Tropical rainforests like the Amazon are home to millions of species, and in a healthy state they absorb vast quantities of planet-warming carbon dioxide, helping to keep the Earth cool.They have come under increasing pressure as a result of decades of deliberate land clearance for agriculture and logging, as well as climate change, which scientists warn can create the conditions for bigger fires to spread.When he took office, President Lula promised to reverse the deforestation which occurred during the presidency of his predecessor, Jair Bolsonaro.Bolsonaro argued that more farming and mining in the Amazon would reduce poverty in the region and environmentalists accused him of allowing deforestation to accelerate.Three years into Bolsonaro's four-year term, the amount of trees cut down in the Amazon rainforest went up by 75%. On his first day in office, on 1 January 2023, Lula insisted that "our goal is to achieve zero deforestation in the Amazon".While that pledge was warmly welcomed by environmentalists, there has been widespread criticism of Lula's decision to approve oil exploration at the mouth of the Amazon river, which activists say flies in the face of his promise to protect the Amazon region.

schema.headline: Brazil's Amazon sees sharp decline of area burned in 2025

title: Lindt's Easter chocolate sales fall after price hike
description: Lindt has partially U-turned on its decision to hike prices after Easter chocolate sales dropped.The Swiss chocolate maker said a "necessary groupwide
body: Lindt has partially U-turned on its decision to hike prices after Easter chocolate sales dropped.The Swiss chocolate maker said a "necessary groupwide" price surge of 11.8% was one of the reasons revenue shrank in the first half of this year, particularly in the UK, Germany, and Switzerland.It also blamed weaker Easter demand and a drop in tourism from Asia and the Middle East "due to geopolitical uncertainties". In response, it said it has adjusted prices and boosted marketing in certain regions for the second half of the year.Around Easter, Lindt is known for its chocolate rabbits wrapped in gold-coloured foil and decorated with a red ribbon and bell on their necks.Overall, the company's sales dipped 0.9%, with European sales down by 2.1%. It added that "performance was impacted by more price-sensitive and mature markets such as Germany, Switzerland and the UK".By volume, meaning the amount of chocolate sold rather than the money it made, overall sales sank 7.5%. Pre-tax profit fell 1.5%.Meanwhile, sales of Lindt chocolate in airports decreased "due to ongoing conflicts in the Middle East, and therefore declining passenger traffic".Lindt said its sales picked up in North America, Australia, China, and Japan, though these countries account for a much smaller slice of its sales than Europe, where it makes over half its revenue.Lindt chief executive Adalbert Lechner said: "The actions we have initiated focus on volume recovery in the second half of 2026 and lay the foundation to regain volume growth momentum in 2027."Lindt is not the only chocolate firm which has been putting prices up. Experts say climate change has led to extreme rainfall and droughts which have decreased cocoa farmers' crops.This pushes up costs of making chocolate, and companies have chosen different ways to react to this, with some reducing chocolate content or sizes rather than raising prices.According to the latest official data, the annual rate of chocolate and sweet price rises is 7.9%, external – much higher than the general rate of UK inflation at 2.8%, external.

schema.headline: Lindt's Easter chocolate sales fall after price hike

title: Scottish Labour leader Anas Sarwar set to become a minister in Burnham's government
description: Sarwar has been Scottish Labour leader since 2021. When Burnham became prime minister on Monday, Sarwar hailed him for his "bold Labour vision".Former
body: Sarwar has been Scottish Labour leader since 2021. When Burnham became prime minister on Monday, Sarwar hailed him for his "bold Labour vision".Former Greater Manchester mayor Burnham appointed his Cabinet on Monday night, but a raft of ministerial appointments are still to come.BBC Scotland Westminster correspondent Paris Gourtsoyannis said Scottish Labour parliamentarians appeared to have been blindsided by the news.An MSP called Sarwar's decision "bizarre", while a senior Scottish Labour MP said it was "absolutely mad. I cannot begin to fathom the logic of it."As a Scottish Labour peer, Sarwar would be entitled to attend meetings of the Scottish PLP at Westminster - a group which earlier this year collectively refused to back their leader's call for Keir Starmer to resign as prime minister.The SNP's deputy leader Keith Brown said: "This is a terrible look for Andy Burnham who waxed lyrical about a new politics and devolving power - he's just pulled the most undemocratic trick in the Westminster book."Anas Sarwar led Scottish Labour to their worst ever election defeat, but as is always the case with the Labour Party, there's always a cushty number in the House of Lords."What a signal to send after their democratic defeat - this is the Labour Party literally lording it over the people of Scotland."Reform UK's Scotland deputy leader Thomas Kerr said: "Only weeks ago, Anas Sarwar stood before the people of Glasgow asking for their trust and insisting that his future was here in Scotland. Today, those same voters will rightly feel betrayed."Throughout the election campaign, he dismissed suggestions that he had ambitions elsewhere and claimed his focus was on serving Scotland."Now, at the first opportunity, he has walked away from the very people who placed their faith in him."Jim Murphy, who was Scottish Labour leader from 2014 to 2015, told BBC News: "If it's confirmed I think it's good news for him [Sarwar] personally but it probably, on balance, strengthens the government."It will open up a vacancy, of course, for leader of the Scottish Labour Party."He said Andy Burnham had a maximum three years until the next election and had appointed people he felt could deliver quickly."He's come to a judgement that Anas, as part of a wider team, can do that," he said."That will be difficult for him [Sarwar]. He will have to get up to speed very quickly but congratulations to him."Murphy added that there was turbulence whenever there was a leader change within a party."I don't enjoy it," he said. "A good group of really talented people were removed from government yesterday but there are always more talented people than jobs available, so Andy will have to work pretty hard delivering what he promised - which is uniting the Labour tribe to enable them to take on Reform and others."

schema.headline: Scottish Labour leader Anas Sarwar set to become a minister in Burnham's government

title: Israeli strikes in Gaza kill 12, including family of six, medics say
description: At least 12 Palestinians, including six members of one family, have been killed in three separate Israeli strikes in the Gaza Strip, according to loca
body: At least 12 Palestinians, including six members of one family, have been killed in three separate Israeli strikes in the Gaza Strip, according to local medics.Video footage showed the family's apartment on fire overnight in the western al-Sabra neighbourhood of Gaza City.The brother of Firas al-Masri told the BBC that he had been burnt to death along with his wife, Salsabeel, and their three daughters and son, Faryal, Salma, Amira and Naim.The Israeli military said its target was a Hamas operative and that it was still looking into the results of the attack.On Tuesday afternoon, another four people were killed in an Israeli air strike on a home in the al-Samar area of central Gaza City, Palestinian media cited medics as saying.Two more were killed in an Israeli drone strike on a vehicle in the al-Zahra area to the south of Gaza City, they added.The Israeli military has not yet commented on those strikes.According to Gaza's Hamas-run health ministry, at least 1,168 Palestinians have been killed in Israeli attacks since a ceasefire between Israel and Hamas took effect last October.Four Israeli soldiers have been killed in Palestinian attacks in Gaza over the same period.The UN's human rights office condemned on Tuesday the recent intensification of Israeli military attacks in Gaza.Between 13 and 20 July, at least 57 Palestinians, including six children and eight women, were killed in strikes on residential buildings, a tent camp for displaced families, and other crowded areas, spokesman Thameen Al-Kheetan said.Thirty-four of those people were "killed far away" from the so-called Yellow Line, which marks territory in Gaza controlled by Israeli forces, he added."Nine months after the announcement of a ceasefire, still, nowhere is safe for Palestinians in Gaza," Al-Kheetan said.He also warned that the killing of civilians "raises concerns of continuing violations of international humanitarian law, war crimes and other possible atrocity crimes in Gaza".The Israeli military has recently announced a series of attacks targeting members of Palestinian armed groups, who it said were involved in the Hamas-led attack on Israel on 7 October 2023, during which about 1,200 people were killed and 251 were taken hostage.Israel responded by launching a massive military campaign in Gaza, during which more than 73,290 people have been killed, according to the territory's health ministry.

schema.headline: Israeli strikes in Gaza kill 12, including family of six, medics say

title: PGA Tour, DP World Tour & Asian Tour alliance puts pressure on LIV
description: LIV Golf has suffered another blow with the announcement of a global alliance between the leading men's professional tours.Masters champion Rory McIlr
body: LIV Golf has suffered another blow with the announcement of a global alliance between the leading men's professional tours.Masters champion Rory McIlroy has welcomed the partnership that the PGA Tour and DP World Tour have struck with the Asian Tour, which had been linked with the LIV circuit.LIV, whose next event starts at the JCB Club in Staffordshire on Thursday, is already reeling from the withdrawal of Saudi Arabian financial backing after the Kingdom ploughed in more than £3.75bn to bankroll its first three years.The PGA Tour and DP World Tour's new partnership with the Asian Tour was confirmed in meetings at last week's Open Championship at Royal Birkdale. "Positive news," McIlroy said in a social media post. "Great to see the respective tours working together for the good of international golf."The PGA and DP World Tours already had a strategic alliance which will now be extended until at least 2029. That deal, which could have expired next year, had been under discussion in recent months.The Asian Tour partnered with LIV in 2021 prior to the launch of the breakaway league the following year. It offered a promotion and relegation pathway to and from LIV's series of $30m tournaments.Under the old deal, the top two from the Asian Tour's order of merit gained promotion to LIV for its 2026 season.The Asian Tour's Saudi-funded International Series also provided lucrative playing opportunities for LIV's leading stars who had been banned by the PGA Tour.They were controversial events because some were staged in Europe, infringing on territory that had previously been the traditional domain of the DP World Tour.Taking immediate effect, the PGA and DP World Tours, through their existing strategic alliance, will partner with the Asian Tour to provide commercial and playing opportunities.There will also be pathways for the leading golfers in Asia to allow them playing rights from the 2027 DP World Tour season.Certain events will return to being co-sanctioned between the DP World and Asian circuits, as was the case prior to the latter's alliance with LIV.This is a blow to LIV boss Scott O'Neil, who is seeking $350m investment to keep alive a circuit that has stars such as Jon Rahm and Bryson DeChambeau playing on it.O'Neil had identified national opens from around the world to play a part in a new schedule and stops on the Asian Tour were considered central to that plan."We feel the time is now right to collaborate with the DP World Tour and PGA Tour to enhance the Asian Tour for our members, fans and partners," said Cho Minn Thant, the Asian Tour's CEO."Bringing the three Tours together under one partnership will increase the bandwidth of the professional game in Asia."Not only will co-sanctioning events see our members competing regularly on the global stage, but we are excited to welcome players from the other tours to our pinnacle events."Ben Cowen, the DP World Tour's chief tournament and operations officer, described the move as "a hugely positive step for golf in Asia, but also an exciting new era of collaboration for our game globally".The PGA Tour's boss of international affairs Christian Hardy added:  "This agreement is another example of what can be accomplished when organisations come together with a shared vision for the future of professional golf."We are excited to welcome the Asian Tour into that effort."

schema.headline: PGA Tour, DP World Tour & Asian Tour alliance puts pressure on LIV

title: Lebanese army says troops deploying in 'pilot zone' after Israeli withdrawal
description: Lebanon's army says its troops are deploying to a "pilot zone" in the south of the country following the withdrawal of Israeli forces under a US-broke
body: Lebanon's army says its troops are deploying to a "pilot zone" in the south of the country following the withdrawal of Israeli forces under a US-brokered agreement reached last month.Engineering units entered the village of Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh in Nabatieh district on Tuesday morning, the army command announced. Later, it said Israeli forces had opened fire in the vicinity of the troops. Israel's military said warning shots had been fired after the troops crossed into the Israeli-declared "security zone".The pilot zone plan should see Israeli troops pulling out of a small part of the territory occupied during their conflict with Hezbollah and the disarmament of the Iran-backed Lebanese group.Tuesday's deployment began ahead of a meeting in Washington between US President Donald Trump and his Lebanese counterpart, Joseph Aoun.Aoun was expected to urge Trump to put pressure on Israel to withdraw from the rest of southern Lebanon and support the Lebanese army in taking full control of areas where Hezbollah holds sway. "I told him [Trump] I need two things from you: political support and supporting the LAF [Lebanese Armed Forces]," Aoun told reporters in the Oval Office. Speaking alongside Aoun, Trump vowed that the US would provide help to Lebanon which had been "hit hard" for decades. "It's been a very badly treated place and country, and we're going to have it properly treated," he said. "And treated with the respect it deserves." The US state department announced on Monday afternoon that "pilot zone operations" had begun in Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh and two other Lebanese villages, Froun and Srifa, in line with the Trilateral Framework agreed by Israel, Lebanon and the US at the end of June.It said the "milestone" was the direct result of discussions last week between Israeli and Lebanese representatives in Rome.Later on Monday, an Israeli military official confirmed the Israel Defense Forces (IDF) would "adjust its force posture in one of the pilot areas" to allow the Lebanese army to carry out its mission, while also "safeguarding the security of IDF soldiers and preserving its right to self-defence and to remove threats"."The objective of the pilot and the agreement is the disarmament of the Hezbollah terrorist organisation and the removal of its illegal weapons," the official said.They also reiterated that the IDF would remain in the "security zone" - the 10km (six-mile) deep strip of occupied Lebanese territory running along the border - for "as long as necessary" to defend northern Israeli communities.On Tuesday, the Lebanese army's command said its units had begun deploying to Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh "based on ongoing communications with the Military Co-ordination Group for Lebanon", referring to the trilateral body created to support implementation of the framework.The statement also called on Lebanese citizens "not to head to the village until the security situation stabilises, and to comply with the instructions of the deployed military units for their own safety".Later on Tuesday, the army command said Israeli forces had "opened fire in the vicinity of these units" and warned that the "attack hinders the implementation of deployment steps in the pilot zones".The IDF said its soldiers had fired "warning shots into the air" after the Lebanese troops and an engineering vehicle "crossed approximately 150m (490ft) into the security zone and breached the barriers" in the neighbouring area of Zawtar al-Sharqiyeh.The Lebanese troops "withdrew from the area, and no injuries were reported", it added.The Lebanese army said its troops were already carrying out missions in nearby Froun and Srifa following Monday's US announcement.An Israeli source also told Haaretz newspaper that there were no Israeli troops in those villages.Also on Tuesday, Lebanon's state-run National News Agency said Israeli forces were behind two large explosions near the villages of Beit Yahoun and Kounine, in Bint Jbeil district. It also reported blasts from Israeli controlled demolitions in the town of Majdal Zoun, in Tyre district.In areas still occupied by Israeli forces, many villages have been completely destroyed by Israeli strikes or demolitions. The Israeli military says it has been destroying Hezbollah infrastructure, but human rights groups say some of what has happened there amounts to the deliberate destruction of civilian infrastructure, a possible war crime.Hezbollah has rejected the agreement between Lebanon's government, Israel and the US, saying the provisions linking Israel's withdrawal to the group's disarmament crossed "all red lines". Israel has said it will not fully withdraw from the country until the group is fully disarmed. According to Trump, the US has "concrete plans" to help Lebanon build the capacity to disarm Hezbollah, but he provided no details."They're also becoming much more self-sufficient," Trump said of the Lebanese army. Additionally, Trump said he would speak directly to representatives of Hezbollah if requested by Aoun. Lebanon was drawn into the war between Israel, the US and Iran on 2 March, when Hezbollah launched rockets into Israel in retaliation for a strike that killed Iran's supreme leader.Israel responded by launching a bombing campaign across Lebanon and invading a significant part of the country's south.More than 4,300 people have been killed in Lebanon, including almost 800 children, women and medics, according to the Lebanese health ministry.Israeli authorities say 37 Israeli soldiers and four civilians have been killed on both sides of the border during the conflict.

schema.headline: Lebanese army says troops deploying in 'pilot zone' after Israeli withdrawal

title: How a former Blue Peter presenter stunned America's Got Talent judges
description: Going into the judges dressing rooms and taking some of their prized possessions might not be the best way of getting them on your side.Smashing those
body: Going into the judges dressing rooms and taking some of their prized possessions might not be the best way of getting them on your side.Smashing those items with a hammer is even less likely to help your chances.Yet that is exactly what Mawhinney did, leaving Mel B, Sofía Vergara and Howie Mandel stunned before revealing that every item had somehow survived.Mandel's phone appeared inside a locked box that had been sitting in front of the judges throughout the audition.The phone then began receiving texts from Mawhinney revealing where the other judges missing valuables had ended up.Vergara's framed picture of her dog was hidden inside her seat cushion, while Mel B's bracelet was concealed in one of the famous Got Talent red buzzers."I don't know how you do it. I believe in magic but I loved it," Mel B said when giving her verdict, which was a yes.Vergara laughed and said the "magic was very impressive". She also voted yes, adding: "I don't know if this was necessary, but it was very exciting."Mendel called Mawhinney "charming, you've got a good look", but it was a no from the comedian, calling the performance "too slow".Simon Cowell agreed on the length of the performance and said "you can't do that on a live show"."Our audience love magic, which is exactly the reason I'm going to say yes."With three yeses, Mawhinney progressed to the next stage and remains in with a chance of winning the cash prize.Mawhinney said that when he entered the judges' dressing rooms he didn't know what he would have ended up with."I knew the plan was to take prized possessions, or whatever I could find, but it just so happened that the jewellery was there," he told BBC Good Morning Ulster programme."It's a hard and set rule that if I'm performing magic, I don't borrow possessions of people."I don't like taking phones. I don't like borrowing rings because I've just heard so many horror stories."But I thought, it's America's Got Talent, I have to do something. So I sort of went for it."The former presenter said producers had been inviting him to audition for America's Got Talent since 2020, but he always felt it sounded like "a massive risk" and his "gut instinct was always no"."But then I left Blue Peter this year and wanted to establish myself as a magician again, because that's what I'm doing now."And I thought, you might as well kick the whole thing off with something a bit extreme, so I'll fly to the States and do that."

schema.headline: How a former Blue Peter presenter stunned America's Got Talent judges

title: World Cup 2026: Argentina react to final defeat and Messi questions
description: Midfielder Enzo Fernandez has claimed Argentina represented their nation "in the best possible way" during the World Cup, despite ugly scenes during a
body: Midfielder Enzo Fernandez has claimed Argentina represented their nation "in the best possible way" during the World Cup, despite ugly scenes during and after their final loss to Spain.Fernandez was sent off late in normal time for two yellow cards and Spain won 1-0 after extra time on Sunday in New Jersey.His first booking was for showing dissent towards Slovenian referee Slavko Vincic and Fernandez was later dismissed for catching Pau Cubarsi with a challenge that sent the defender cartwheeling in the air.But, in a statement on social media, the Chelsea midfielder did not mention the red card and said: "As time goes by, you realise there is something much bigger than just a result."For years, this group has represented the team in the best possible way. It teaches that competing isn't just about winning; it's about giving your all for the jersey and never giving up."It has been a privilege to be part of a group that always stepped up, competed at the highest level, and defended these colours with pride, humility, and commitment."While the match ended with ugly scenes broadcast around the world, questions remain over what happened behind closed doors prior to kick-off.There has been widespread speculation on social media about what was said in the Argentina dressing room before the game, including whether Lionel Messi informed his team-mates that the final would be his last match for the national side.It comes after Argentine television company RLC Noticias posted footage appearing to show Messi urging his team-mates to stay calm on their walk to the tunnel - and "forget about everything else".In the footage, which has not been verified by the BBC, Messi is seen saying: "Let's go, boys, [let's keep] calm people, calm. The main thing is for us to remain calm, boys, that we remain calm."Let's just concentrate on playing, nothing else - calm, OK? Forget everything else."Let's just play, let's just concentrate on playing. Let's go."The actions of the Argentina players on the pitch were heavily criticised by former England internationals.Ex-England captain Alan Shearer said: "Paredes threw two or three haymakers into someone's face. He went for them. There is no place or space for that."We know how much it means to them and we know how much it hurts to lose, but there is a way to lose. Too many times we have seen that from Argentina. The reaction after the final whistle is terrible."Former England goalkeeper Joe Hart added: "The game's done, they can have absolutely no complaints about what happened. Disgusting behaviour."Argentina manager Lionel Scaloni attempted to calm the situation down in his post-match interview.He said: "We are gracious in victory, and we must be gracious in defeat. Today, we are showing that we know how to lose."We lost the match, and we accept it, but that doesn't mean we stop living or forget everything we did to get here."Argentina, the World Cup winners in 1978, 1986 and 2022, won their first seven matches in North America, beating Algeria, Austria, Jordan, Cape Verde, Egypt, Switzerland and England before losing to Spain.Against Egypt, Argentina were 2-0 down before scoring three late goals to win and in the semi-final with England, the Three Lions led 1-0 until the 85th minute before goals from Fernandez and a Lautaro Martinez header gave them a 2-1 win.Several media outlets in Argentina have suggested the final could be Scaloni's last in charge and called it the end of an era, with talisman Lionel Messi, in tears at the end, unlikely to play at another World Cup."The pain is immense, and this wound will take time to heal. But I also hold onto all the good things," Messi wrote on social media.

schema.headline: World Cup 2026: Argentina react to final defeat and Messi questions

title: Your tips on getting a fussy child to eat, and six recipes they'll love
description: Image source, Getty ImagesByPhilippa RoxbyHealth reporterIt's the start of the summer holidays and you've already had five battles with your child to
body: Image source, Getty ImagesByPhilippa RoxbyHealth reporterIt's the start of the summer holidays and you've already had five battles with your child to get them to eat a bit of broccoli. For many parents, these few weeks can be a stressful time of the year at the dinner table. So how should we deal with our picky eaters? The BBC asked you for your tips on making mealtimes a more enjoyable experience.Encourage eating veg by changing how you serve themMum-of-four Natalia, from south-west London, makes veggie nuggets for her children."They like green beans but not by themselves, so we just fry them in a little bit of butter and dip in breadcrumbs. The same with cauliflower and lots of other vegetables," she says.Teacher Kieron Fisher, who lives in Newbury, Berkshire, includes vegetables in meals his six-year-old twins Kit and Ollie already enjoy, such as curries and spaghetti Bolognese."It helps increase their vegetable intake without causing a battle."Image source, Kieron FisherImage caption, Kieron's twins Kit and Ollie can be "quite fussy at times" but picnicking in the park is always a hitEmma Robins, from Reading, says broccoli hidden in pesto pasta is a favourite for her three-year-old son Alfred, as well as veg pulsed into pasta sauces. She also recommends making your own burgers with vegetables.A lack of routine in the holidays can be particularly challenging for some children, so Kieron suggests using visual boards showing the healthy snack they are about to have and another showing the activity they are going to do next.Children with special educational needs can sometimes experience sensory difficulties when it comes to food - and are not classified as fussy eaters. Some of these suggestions may not be suitable for them.Don't force them to finish everything on their plate"If they're hungry, they'll eat. I don't force them to because I think that can instil pressure," says Doone, from London. She says her daughter Cora, two, used to be the "best eater" but seeing others at nursery turn their noses up at certain foods made her do the same."I'm trying not to make a big deal of it. Even though they don't eat it, I always put the broccoli on the plate so that they're seeing it."Emma suggests giving lots of options, so that kids think they are getting to choose. In Alfred's lunchbox, she always adds two or three healthy options.And she says to praise them when they take an interest. "If they say one day they want carrots, run and get the carrots and give big praise when they eat them," Emma says.Keep offering food until they try it  It may take up to 15 attempts before kids learn to like new foods, research suggests."Keep going," says one mum outside Barnes Primary School, in south-west London. "Keep offering it and they will pick it up and try it. We try to make everyone try everything in our house, even the tiniest bit."Sometimes it can be the colour or texture of a certain food that puts them off, another parent says, until they actually try it.Children refusing to eat new foods or rejecting ones they've always liked is not unusual. More than half will show fussy eating habits at some point, the NHS says, external.Vegetables, in particular, can taste quite bitter to young palates. Most habits will improve over time.But if your kids start to avoid certain foods and limit how much they eat in an unhealthy way, it's worth seeking medical advice, external.Make eating a family affair - and get the portion size right"I always eat with my girls and I eat what they're eating," says Doone, who has two "quite fussy eaters".The British Nutrition Foundation agrees that food should be a family affair - allowing children to learn from what their parents do. When a bit older, they can help with preparation too.Emma adds to the fun by taking her kids fruit picking and also by letting them choose at the supermarket, then having tasty picnics in the garden.Image source, Getty ImagesImage caption, Nutritionists says food should be a family affair - allowing children to learn from what their parents doBut first thing's first, says Kieron: think about the portion size."I make sure their plates aren't too full or overcrowded, as too much food at once can feel overwhelming," he says.Serve snacks alongside healthier options Doone says: "I am making a conscious effort not to give many snacks because then they're more hungry at dinner time."Natalia leaves out healthy finger food, like chopped peppers and apples, for her children to pick at.Image caption, Finger food is easy to pick at Don't completely ban them, says Kieron - just serve snacks alongside healthier options, like berries or other fruit, replicate snack times at school and don't stress about the odd treat."Six weeks [of summer holidays] is a long time, so it's about focusing on the overall balance of their diet rather than aiming for perfection every day," he says.

schema.headline: Your tips on getting a fussy child to eat, and six recipes they'll love

title: Viral TikTok Brombil Reservoir owners warn visitors to stay away
description: Owners of a "blue lagoon" that went viral on TikTok during the Covid pandemic have warned people not to enter the site, particularly during the curren
body: Owners of a "blue lagoon" that went viral on TikTok during the Covid pandemic have warned people not to enter the site, particularly during the current hot weather.Brombil Reservoir in Margam in Neath Port Talbot attracted thousands of visitors, which caused traffic problems and littering.To reach the 30m-deep (100ft) reservoir, visitors must walk up a steep 900m (2,953ft) public footpath.Leisure Estate Investments Ltd, which bought the secluded site at auction for £20,000 in 2024, are now charging £2 a visit as "a temporary deterrent" and have installed barriers on its dam face.It is concerned about people swimming, paddleboarding, littering and damaging structures around the site.Although the reservoir can be accessed via public footpaths, the barriers were installed to stop people from walking on the dam face - but these have since been removed by trespassers.The owners said the £2 levy was introduced in an effort to manage unauthorised visits and help fund clean-up operations, as part of its efforts to discourage public access because of "inherent safety risks".A spokesperson for the company said: "We have strictly banned [people] from going there and walking on the face of the dam."Our long-term vision has always been to build a sustainable public-private framework so the community can safely enjoy this asset."However, managing a critical piece of infrastructure presents immense structural, environmental, and financial challenges that cannot be indefinitely sustained by a private entity alone."The reservoir is a flooded 19th Century colliery that was repurposed to supply water to Port Talbot Docks, and is now famous for its striking turquoise colour.It plays a vital role in flood protection, helping shield downstream residential areas, and has a "unique aquatic ecosystem" that has faced "severe environmental degradation caused by fly-tipping and littering", Brombil Reservoir Ltd said.After the landmark went viral on a TikTok video with more than one million views, nearby residents claimed they had been "suffering" because of the traffic and litter problems. To reach the 30m-deep (100ft) reservoir, visitors must walk up a steep 900m (2,953ft) public footpath, which is also the only way down. In an effort to combat vandalism, including the destruction of iron security gates on deep-water chambers and persistent littering, the company introduced the £2 charge.It said it was to "manage unauthorised access and fund clean-up operations. It is not a commercial ticket".The company added its goal was to safely open the site for public use, saying it recognised the benefits of "blue spaces" for health and well-being.Since taking ownership in 2024, Brombil Reservoir Ltd said it had invested in structural surveys, engineering assessments, landscaping and waste clearance.It added that compliance requirements from Natural Resources Wales (NRW) for high-risk dams had increased significantly, requiring tens of thousands of pounds in ongoing safety investment.Matthew O'Brien of NRW's reservoir safety group said the Reservoirs Act 1975, meant owners were responsible "for ensuring their reservoirs are properly maintained and managed". He added that these rules were designed "to ensure that reservoirs are managed safely and help reduce the risk of flooding to people and property downstream". Owners of regulated reservoirs pay an annual fee of £276 towards NRW's regulatory costs, external. The company said it had repeatedly sought support from Neath Port Talbot council and local politicians to help fund and preserve the reservoir, but had received no response.It added: "We genuinely want to work constructively with Neath Port Talbot council, NRW, and our political representatives to deliver a safe, biodiverse, and secure site."Brombil Reservoir Ltd warned that without public sector support or a partnership agreement, it could be forced to "decommission the reservoir".It added: "This would unfortunately eliminate the area's primary flood defence and destroy a vital ecological habitat. "We sincerely hope local authorities will join us at the table to find a sustainable, shared way forward."The council said it had"offered advice where appropriate" to the reservoir owners.  "As the site is privately owned, decisions regarding its operation and investment are a matter for the owner," it added. "Any future arrangements should ensure that visitor activity is managed responsibly and does not place undue pressure on local communities."We remain open to constructive dialogue with the owner."

schema.headline: Viral TikTok Brombil Reservoir owners warn visitors to stay away
